coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom doped carbon dots with nanoenzyme like properties as theranostic platforms for free radical scavenging, imaging, and chemotherapy

Chiu, Sheng-Hui
Lin, Jin-Sheng
Girma, Wubshet Mekonnen
Ling, Yong-Chien
Chang, Jia-Yaw
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon-based artificial nanoenzymes have gained increasing interest as emerging and promising nanotheranostic agents due to their biocompatibility, low cost, and straightforward production. Herein, a multifunctional Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots (MnNS:CDs) nanoenzyme exhibiting scavenging activity against reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), photoluminescence quantum yield of 17.7%, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast was explored. The optical, magnetic, and antioxidant properties of MnNS:CDs were then regulated by control over Mn incorporation to achieve higher photostability and antioxidant properties. Furthermore, conjugation of MnNS:CDs with hyaluronic acid (HA) (denoted as MnNS:CDs@HA) endowed them with high biocompatibility, which is validated by in vivo studies on zebrafish, and the ability to specifically target cluster determinant 44 (CD44)-overexpressing B16F1 cells, as verified by in vitro confocal and MRI studies. The MnNS:CDs@HA probe with therapeutic antioxidant and dual-modal imaging capability was further assessed for non-covalent binding of doxorubicin (DOX) as a model chemotherapeutic cancer drug. Results showed that targeted delivery and pH-dependent release of DOX elicited apparent cell toxicity (90%) toward B16F1 cancer cells when compared to free DOX treatment group (60%). Benefiting from their intrinsic antioxidant properties, and dual-modal imaging ability, the MnNS:CDs@HA nanocarrier is projected to improve non-invasive targeted diagnosis and therapy.
Statement of significance Carbon dots (CDs) have gained increasing interest as emerging and promising artificial functional nanomaterials that mimic the structures and functions of natural enzymes. In this work, Mn, N, and S incorporated CDs (MnNS:CDs) were synthesized using a one-pot microwave hydrothermal method to serve as fluorescent and magnetic resonance imaging probes, and catalase mimics in the reduction of the oxidative-stress related damage. Further conjugation of the probes with hyaluronic acid endows them with a good in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility as well as the capability to selectively target CD44-overexpressing cancer cells, as investigated by in vitro fluorescence, and magnetic resonance imaging. The dual-modal nanoprobe was then used to carry on doxorubicin through a non-covalent association. Favorably, targeted delivery, and pH-responsive release of doxorubicin enhanced cell killing efficiency by 50% as opposed to the free doxorubicin treatment group. The presented theranostic heteroatom doped CDs hold great promise for dual-modal imaging enabling accurate diagnosis coupled with therapeutic effect through free radical scavenging and chemotherapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation of the synthesis of MnNS:CDs, using microwave irradiation. (B) and (C) TEM images and size distribution, (D) energy dispersive spectroscopy, and (E) Raman spectrum of MnNS:CDs. MnNS:CDs, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots.

(A) UV-visible absorbance, (B) PL emission (excitation wavelength = 360 nm) of MnNS:CDs samples prepared by using different Mn precursor solutions. (C) MnNS:CDs PL mapping. (D) PL decay lifetime, and (E) PL stability over time for NS:CDs and MnNS:CDs. (F) Relative PL intensity of MnNS:CDs at elevated NaCl concentrations monitored for 72 h. MnNS:CDs, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots; PL, Photoluminescence. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) XPS survey and high-resolution (B) C 1 s, (C) O 1 s, and (D) Mn 2p XPS spectra of MnNS:CDs. (E) EPR spectra of MnNS:CDs measured at 10 K, with various Mn concentrations: S1 (0.01 mmol), S2 (0.02 mmol), S3 (0.03 mmol), and S4 (0.04 mmol). (F) T1- and T2-weighted spin-eco images of MnNS:CDs at various concentrations. (G) Water-proton longitudinal (1/T1) and transverse (1/T2) relaxation rates of MnNS:CDs measured as a function of different concentrations of Mn, using a 7 T MRI scanner. PL, Photoluminescence; MnNS:CDs; Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; EPR, electron paramagnetic resonance. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV−vis absorption spectra of DPPH solutions after mixing with 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50 µg/mL of (A) NS:CDs, and (B) MnNS:CDs. (C) Inhibition value of DPPH radical of h-CDs versus ascorbic acid. UV−vis absorption spectra of TMB solutions after mixing with 0 to 50 µg/mL of (D) NS:CDs, and (E) MnNS:CDs. (F) Inhibition values of •OH by h-CDs versus ascorbic acid. (G) UV−vis absorption spectra of ONOO− solution after the addition of 100 µg/mL MnNSCDs from 0 to 30 min with 3 min interval. (H) UV−vis absorption spectra of ONOO− solution after 30 min reaction with 0 to 100 µg/mL MnNSCDs. Time-dependent OD at 302 nm of ONOO−solution treated with different concentrations of MnNSCDs. Error bars indicate the standard deviation from three independent replicates (n = 3). DPPH, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl; MnNS:CDs; Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots; TMB, 3 3′ 5 5′-tetramethylbenzidine.

(A) Schematic illustration of MnNS:CDs conjugation with HA. (B) UV-visible absorption, (C) FT-IR spectra, (D) and (E) DLS, and (F) zeta potential study of MnNS:CDs and MnNS:CDs@HA aqueous solutions. MnNS:CDs@HA, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots conjugated with hyaluronic acid; FT-IR, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy; DLS, Dynamic light scattering. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Cell viability results for B16F1 cell after incubation with MnNS:CDs@HA at different concentrations (0, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 µg/mL) for 24, 48, and 72 h at 37 °C using WST-1 assay. B16F1 cells incubated with PBS were used as control (0 µg/mL). (B) Survival and hatching rate of zebrafish embryos exposed to different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 µg/mL) of MnNS:CDs@HA. (C) Cytoprotective effect of MnNS:CDs@HA against H2O2-induced toxicity of B16F1. Cells were incubated with MnNS:CDs@HA for 24 h and then treated with 0.2 and 1 mM H2O2 for 90 min. Cells without treatment were used as control. Data represent the mean ± standard deviation of three experiments. (D) Proposed pathways of ROS scavenging activity against H2O2-induced oxidative stress. ROS, reactive oxygen species; MnNS:CDs@HA, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots conjugated with hyaluronic acid.

B16F1 intracellular ROS generation evaluation by DCFH-DA. DAPI fluorescence images collected in the range of 409–570 nm and DCFH fluorescence images captured in the range of 485–535 nm. Green fluorescence indicates the presence of ROS, and blue fluorescence shows stained cell nuclei. Intracellular ROS generation was illustrated for pre-treated B16F1 cells with 200 and 500 µg/mL MnNS:CDs@HA. B16F1 cell without treatment and treated with DECFH-DA only were used as a negative control. Two groups treated with H2O2 were used as positive control groups. All the scale bars represent 20 µm. DCFH-DA, 2´,7´-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate; DAPI, 4,6´‐diamidino‐2‐phenylindole; ROS, reactive oxygen species; MnNS:CDs@HA, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots conjugated with hyaluronic acid. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Confocal fluorescent images of B16F1 and HeLa cells incubated with MnNS:CDs@HA, fixed, and stained with NucView agent. For the HA-pretreated group, B16F1 cells incubated with free HA (300 µg/mL) for 2 h, and then incubated with MnNS:CDs@HA for 24 h at 37 °C. NucView channel (green emission) labeled cell nuclei, MnNS:CDs@HA channel (blue emission) represents the location of MnNS:CDs@HA, overlay imaging indicates the combination of two channels. (B) T1-weighted MR images of MnNS:CDs at various concentrations in B16F1 and HeLa cells. (C) Mechanism of MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX synthesis and schematic illustration of B16F1 cellular internalization via CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis and intercellular pH-responsive release of DOX. (D) pH-sensitive drug release profile of MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX in PBS buffer at pH 7.4, 6.0, or 5.0. (E) Viability of B16F1 cells after treatment with MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX and free DOX. All the scale bars represent 20 µm. Error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3). Significant differences at the same drug concentration at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001 are indicated. MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX, Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots conjugated with hyaluronic acid and loaded with doxorubicin (DOX); PBS, phosphate-buffered saline. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Heteroatom doped carbon dots with nanoenzyme like properties as theranostic platforms for free radical scavenging, imaging, and chemotherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based artificial nanoenzymes have gained increasing interest as emerging and promising nanotheranostic agents due to their biocompatibility, low cost, and straightforward production. Herein, a multifunctional Mn, N, and S incorporated carbon dots (MnNS:CDs) nanoenzyme exhibiting scavenging activity against reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), photoluminescence quantum yield of 17.7%, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast was explored. The optical, magnetic, and antioxidant properties of MnNS:CDs were then regulated by control over Mn incorporation to achieve higher photostability and antioxidant properties. Furthermore, conjugation of MnNS:CDs with hyaluronic acid (HA) (denoted as MnNS:CDs@HA) endowed them with high biocompatibility, which is validated by in vivo studies on zebrafish, and the ability to specifically target cluster determinant 44 (CD44)-overexpressing B16F1 cells, as verified by in vitro confocal and MRI studies. The MnNS:CDs@HA probe with therapeutic antioxidant and dual-modal imaging capability was further assessed for non-covalent binding of doxorubicin (DOX) as a model chemotherapeutic cancer drug. Results showed that targeted delivery and pH-dependent release of DOX elicited apparent cell toxicity (90%) toward B16F1 cancer cells when compared to free DOX treatment group (60%). Benefiting from their intrinsic antioxidant properties, and dual-modal imaging ability, the MnNS:CDs@HA nanocarrier is projected to improve non-invasive targeted diagnosis and therapy.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained increasing interest as emerging and promising artificial functional nanomaterials that mimic the structures and functions of natural enzymes. In this work, Mn, N, and S incorporated CDs (MnNS:CDs) were synthesized using a one-pot microwave hydrothermal method to serve as fluorescent and magnetic resonance imaging probes, and catalase mimics in the reduction of the oxidative-stress related damage. Further conjugation of the probes with hyaluronic acid endows them with a good in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility as well as the capability to selectively target CD44-overexpressing cancer cells, as investigated by in vitro fluorescence, and magnetic resonance imaging. The dual-modal nanoprobe was then used to carry on doxorubicin through a non-covalent association. Favorably, targeted delivery, and pH-responsive release of doxorubicin enhanced cell killing efficiency by 50% as opposed to the free doxorubicin treatment group. The presented theranostic heteroatom doped CDs hold great promise for dual-modal imaging enabling accurate diagnosis coupled with therapeutic effect through free radical scavenging and chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as interesting fluorescent materials for a broad range of biomedical applications owing to their biocompatibility, tunable excitation and emission wavelengths, photostability, and scalability [1-4]. Structurally, CDs are defined as discrete, zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanoparticles consist of cores with sp 2 conjugated carbon surrounded by amorphous shells with numerous surface functionalities, such as carboxyl, amine, hydroxyl, and carbonyl moieties [1,5]. Regarding the optical properties of carbon nanomaterials, π-states of the sp 2 sites are determining factors and their surface defect-derived visible fluorescence emissions originate from the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the strongly localized π and π* electronic levels of the sp 2 sites lie between the bandgap of the σ and σ* states of the sp 3 matrix [6]. As the surface defects become more stable via surface passivation or functionalization, higher radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes as well as more radiant fluorescence emissions can be achieved. The most common method for enhancing photoluminescence (PL) or inducing multicolor emission is doping of nitrogen [7,8], sulfur [9], boron [10], silicon [11], and phosphorus [12], into the lattices of carbon. The improved PL and attractive optical characteristics of heteroatom-doped CDs (h-CDs) were attributed to the electron-withdrawing ability of dopants or a new energy level in the electronic structure as well as to their role in the passivation of defects [6,9]. However, even h-CDs generally exhibit a lower quantum yield (QY) than that of the conventional semiconductor quantum dots owing to the presence of oxygen functionalities, such as hydroxyl and epoxy groups, which may result in non-radiative recombination of excitons [13,14]. For example, dopants’ active sites endowed CDs with efficient electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen photocatalytic reduction reaction [15]. N and S co-doped CDs (NS:CDs) were also reported for both higher performances in PL [2,4] and photovoltaic reactions [16,17]. Interestingly, these electron-donating doped sites, along with surficial functional groups, can destruct free radicals, providing h-CDs imaging probes with antioxidant properties [18-20].

It is generally accepted that most of the degenerative diseases afflicting humanity originated from the deleterious effect of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) [21]. In the normal chemical reactions taking place inside human bodies, free radicals are generated by various endogenous systems to eradicate harmful bacteria and toxins through transferring energy. An imbalance between the systemic manifestation of ROS and cells’ antioxidant defenses creates an oxidative stress state which accelerates cell aging and causes several devastating diseases [22]. Although the concern over excessive free radicals has drawn a great deal of attention and provoked intense research interest, however, owing to increasing air pollution, usage of unhealthy food additives, inferior cosmetics, and smoking, the risk of exposure to free radicals is ever-growing. A broad spectrum of non-enzymatic and enzymatic antioxidants like glutathione peroxidase, superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione S-Transferase, and N-acetylcysteine (NAC) has been widely applied to curb and combat oxidative stress-related symptoms [23]. While these natural products exhibit high catalytic efficiency and substrate specificity, nevertheless, disadvantages such as instability, high cost of purification, and sensitivity of catalytic activity to the physiological conditions constrained their antioxidant applications [24]. In this regard, the development of nanomaterials mimicking the radical scavenging function of enzymes so-called “Nanoenzyme” attracts growing interest due to better stability and tunable enzyme-like activity.

Recently, carbon nanomaterials, including single-wall carbon nanotube [25], graphene materials [18,19,26,27], CDs [28,29], and fullerene [30] have been explored for free radical scavenging activities. While fullerenes antioxidant properties are attributed to their high electron affinity arising from their unique molecular structures, the structural defects of CNTs are proposed to endow the ROS scavenging function. Yet, the application of these materials in the human body is hindered by their hydrophobic nature and low biocompatibility. Favorably, water-soluble h-CDs are proved effective in scavenging ROS, including hydroxyl (•OH), superoxide anion superoxide (O2 •–), and RNS such as 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) nitric oxide (•NO) and peroxynitrite (ONOO–) [18,19,31].

In a systematic study, Qiu et al. suggested that the main scavenging sites of graphene oxide are associated with the sp 2-carbon network rather than surficial oxygen groups [19]. Recently, Nilewski et al. reported highly oxidized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from coal, which were mimics of the superoxide dismutase. Chemical structural and antioxidant activities of the GQDs were compared with graphene oxide and hydrophilic carbon clusters, and it has suggested that the difference in the quinones and carboxyl groups has led to the variation in scavenging effect [32]. Diamine-functionalized GQDs also exhibited radical scavenging and Fe(II) and Cu(II) chelating properties attributed to their sp 2-carbon domains as well as amine and carboxylate functional groups present at the surface of CDs [33]. Hui et al. recommended that the antioxidant properties of CDs are more influenced by the type and amount of heteroatoms rather than by the surface area and defects [27]. Accordingly, it is important to investigate the role of dopants on the antioxidant properties and determine the optimum PL emission and ROS scavenging effect in order to improve the multifunctionality of h-CDs for biomedical applications.

The incorporation of Mn2+ and Gd3+paramagnetic ions into CDs offers a straightforward approach to obtain contrast agents for dual-modal PL/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [34,35]. Liu et al. reported Gd3+doped CDs loaded with a photosensitizer to construct a multifunctional platform for MRI/PL dual imaging and antitumor therapy [36]. GQDs incorporated with Fe3O4 and SiO2 have also prepared and explored for in vitro MRI and fluorescence imaging and the intracellular release of doxorubicin (DOX) by Su et al. [37]. Motivated by promising applications of h-CDs, it is instructive to explore them for the development of multi-modal nanoprobes with a therapeutic effect in the form of free radical scavenging to achieve safer and more effective theranostic contrast agents.

Herein, we report a rapid one-pot microwave-assisted method for preparing luminescent h-CDs incorporated with Mn denoted as MnNS:CDs, and investigation of their optical, MRI, and antioxidant properties. The as-prepared MnNS:CDs retain not only good fluorescence emission but also contrast effect for T1 and T2 MR imaging, which make them promising dual-modal fluorescence/MR imaging probes. Furthermore, the obtained MnNS:CDs probe was conjugated with hyaluronic acid (HA) and loaded with DOX to construct the MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX nanocarrier for targeted chemotherapy.

Citric acid trisodium salt (SC, 98%), 3 3′ 5 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99%), N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC, 99%), 2,2´-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) (EBEA, 98%), 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES, 99.5%), Eagle's minimum essential medium (EMEM), 2´,7´-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT, 98%), β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH), phenazine methosulfate(PMS, 90%), and 4,6´‐diamidino‐2‐phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 99%), iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeO4S•7H2O, 99%), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (Sulfo-NHS, 97%), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 97%), manganese (II) acetate anhydrous (98%), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were obtained from Alfa-Aesar. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (98%) was purchased from Acros. Sodium hyaluronate (research-grade, 100–150 kDa) and DOX (99%) were purchased from Nacalai Tesque, Fusol material Co., LTD, and Lifecore Biomedical, respectively. The tetrazolium salt WST-1 (4-[3-(4-Iodophenyl)−2-[4-nitrophenyl]−2H-5-tetrazolio]−1,3-benzene disulfonate) was purchased from Roche Diagnostics. All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade.

MnNS:CDs were synthesized by rapid hydrothermal treatment using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal process. Briefly, SC (0.16 mmol), NAC (0.24 mmol), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (0.16 mmol), and manganese (II) acetate (0.02 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (15 mL) into a glass vessel, and the reaction proceeded at 220 °C for 10 min in an Anton Paar single-mode microwave reactor. The mixture was then cooled to ambient temperature, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm filter paper. The obtained solution was subsequently purified using a 1 K molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) centrifugal device (MacrosepⓇAdvance) at 2000 rpm for 1 h and the MnNS:CDs solution was dried in a freeze dryer. The NS:CDs were synthesized following the same procedure without the addition of the Mn precursor.

The MnNS:CDs were conjugated with HA via the formation of amide bonds between the carboxylic surface functional groups of MnNS:CDs with NH2-functionalized HA amine groups. Typically, HA (1.22 × 10−2 mmol) was dissolved in MES buffer solution (4 mL) followed by the addition of EDC (5.2 × 10−2 mmol) and Sulfo-NHS (4.6 × 10−2 mmol). The mixture was then stirred for 30 min for the activation of carboxylic groups. Subsequently, 0.068 mmol of EBEA was added to the activated HA solution and stirred for 18 h. Then, the amine-functionalized HA solution was purified using a centrifuge device (3 K MWCO). Next, 5.2 × 10−2 mmol EDC and 4.6 × 10−2 mmol Sulfo-NHS in 1 mL MES were added to 20 mg of MnNS:CDs and stirred for 30 min to activate their carboxylic groups. Finally, the activated MnNS:CDs and the amine-functionalized HA solution were stirred and reacted for 12 h to obtain the MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates. Further purification of MnNS:CDs@HA was conducted by centrifugation (3 K MWCO) at 6000 rpm for 20 min to remove unreacted chemicals. The obtained MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates were freeze-dried and stored in the dark for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cell viability tests
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse melanoma (B16F1) cancer cells, and human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) were cultivated in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Hyclone), 1% non-essential amino acids, 1% sodium pyruvate, 1% l-glutamine, 1% antibiotic antimycotic formulation, and 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C, in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. The cytotoxicity of MnNS:CDs@HA was then assessed in vitro using WST-1 assay. Cells (~1.0 × 104/well) were seeded in a 96-well plate. After 24 h, the medium was washed three times and added 0.1 mL fresh culture medium contains various concentrations (0, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 µg/mL) of MnNS:CDs@HA and the cells were cultured for additional 24, h followed by washing with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) 3 times. Next, the medium in each well was replaced with the fresh medium (0.01 mL of WST-1 and 0.1 mL culture medium). The plate was incubated for another 2 h at 37 °C. Cell viability was monitored by measuring light absorbance at 450 nm using a microplate reader (BioTek, Synergy H1, USA).

The antioxidant properties of NS:CD, MnNS:CD against DPPH and OH• radicals were studied according to previous reports with slight modification [38,39]. For the DPPH assay, different concentrations (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50 µg/mL) of the samples’ aqueous solution (200 µL) were mixed with DPPH (2 mL, 100 µM in methanol) solution. Upon reaction of DPPH with a radical-scavenger, a reduced and stable DPPHH complex is formed, leading to a decrease in DPPH absorption at 515 nm [40]. After 60 min reaction in the dark at room temperature, the inhibition percentage (%) was estimated using the Eq. (1):
(1)
Inhibitio % =
(
A DPPH
−
A Sample
)
/
A DPPH
× 100 %
where A represents the solution absorbance at 515 nm with and without h-CDs samples.

In the case of •OH scavenging activity assay, 1 mL aqueous solution of samples was mixed with the TMB solution having 50 µL of TMB (10 mM in DMSO), 100 µL FeSO47H2O (10 mM in DI water), 750 µL H2O2 (100 mM in DI water), and 100 µL PBS buffer (pH 4.0). The solution absorbance at 652 nm was then monitored, and free radical scavenging activity was estimated using Eq. (1) exchanging the TMB solution absorbance with that of the DPPH. For the assessment of O2 •− scavenging activity, an assay based on the reduction of NBT in the presence of NADH and PMS was employed. The 2 mL reaction mixture contained 50 µM NBT, 78 µM NADH, 0.02 M PBS, and different concentrations of MnNS:CDs. Upon the addition of 15 µL PMS (1 M) under aerobic conditions, the absorbance change was recorded at 560 nm for 3 min [41].

For evaluation of ONOO– scavenging activity, the ONOO ions were generated by the reaction of H2O2 with nitrite. 10 mL of NaNO2 (50 mM) and H2O2 (25 mM) solutions were mixed for 3 min in an ice bath. Then, 5 mL HCl (1 M) and 5 mL NaOH (1.5 M) were successively and quickly added into the stirring mixture. The ONOO−scavenging ability of MnNS:CDs nanoenzyme examined by monitoring the decrease in absorbance of ONOO− at 302 nm [42].

Furthermore, DCFH-DA was applied to assess the level of intracellular ROS. B16F1 cells in a 6-well plate were first incubated for 24 h with MnNS:CDs@HA, followed by incubation with H2O2 for 90 min. Afterward, cells were washed with PBS and cultured with DCFH-DA for 1 h. The cells were then washed twice with PBS and stained with DAPI (10 µg/mL) for 20 min. The fluorescence images were examined by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica TCS SP2).

B16F1 and HeLa cells were incubated with MnNS:CDs@HA (500 µg/mL) for 24 h. Subsequently, non-internalized MnNS:CDs@HA were washed with PBS and cells were fixed with 75% alcohol (2 mL). The nuclei were stained by Nucview green in which 2 mL of its solution (0.05 µg/mL in PBS) were cultured with fixed cells. After 20 min, cells were washed with PBS, and cell images were captured by CLSM.

Cells were cultured with 5 mL medium into a 50 mL serum bottle for 24 h. Next, the culture medium was exchanged with a fresh medium comprising different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA. After incubation for 24 h, cells were trypsinized, washed, and re-suspended in PBS. The MR images of cells inside 0.2 mL tubes were taken on a Bruker MedSpec 7T MRI system with a birdcage head coil. The Mn concentration was estimated by using inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Horiba JY2000–2).

The anticancer drug (DOX) was loaded into MnNS:CDs@HA, termed MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX, by magnetically stirring the mixtures of 9.20 × 10−3 mmol of DOX in PBS (5 mL, pH 7.4) with MnNS:CDs@HA (1 mg/mL, 5 mL) at room temperature and in the dark for 24 h. The unloaded DOX was separated by applying the centrifugal filter (Vivaspin, 3 K MWCO) at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The concentration of unloaded DOX was measured by recording absorbance at 490 nm relative to the generated DOX standard curve. The loading efficiency was then calculated and expressed in percentage according to Eq. (2):

The DOX release from MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX was quantified by dispersing MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX in PBS at pH 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4 and under continuous stirring at 37 °C. The solutions were taken at selected time intervals, and released DOX was determined as mentioned above. Finally, the releasing efficiency was calculated by Eq. (3) and expressed in percentage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo cytotoxicity using zebrafish embryo
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo cytotoxicity experiments were conducted by using wild-type and AB strains of male and female zebrafish (Danio rerio). Male to female fishes with a ratio of 2:1 were taken in a tank for zebrafish mating. They were kept in an aquarium at 28 °C with controlled conditions of 10 h/13 h light/dark. Ten Zebrafish embryos were incubated with different concentrations of the sample solution. The embryonic developmental stages were evaluated at different hours post-fertilization (hpf) by using an inverted microscope (Olympus, IX73) armed with a digital camera. The fertilized embryos were exposed to MnNS:CDs@HA at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 µg/mL). The treatment of each group was performed using ten embryos for all experiments and was done in three replicate measurements. The embryonic survival rate was calculated using the percentage of viable embryos with respect to the total number of embryos at 96 hpf. The embryonic hatching percentage was calculated using the percentage of completely hatched embryos over the total number of embryos at 96 hpf. Animal experiments were in accordance with the guidelines on the use and care of laboratory animals for biomedical research published by the National Institute of Health, and all procedures and protocols were reviewed and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of National Tsing Hua University.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A drop of the sample solution is pipetted onto a TEM copper grid, and it was directly observed once the medium was evaporated at room temperature. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorbance and PL spectra were recorded by using Jasco V-630 and Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometers, respectively. Quinine sulfate (QY = 95%) used as a reference standard, and PL QY was calculated as previously reported [43]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo VG Scientific) was used to study chemical and electronic states of the elements. Al Kα (1486 eV) was used as the X-ray excitation source, and CasaXPS software was used for the quantification analyses. Raman spectra were taken with laser excitation wavelengths of 532 nm using a UniG2D equipped with a microscope system. Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer used to obtain Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Bruker Elexsys E-580 spectrometer to perform electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments. Zetasizer 2000 MALVERN was used to measure the zeta potential. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of 1 mg/mL aqueous solutions of samples were performed at a 90° angle using a Malvern instrument. 7T Bruker BioSpec 70/30 imaging spectrometer was used to acquire MRI.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine statistical significance. A difference was considered to be statistically significant at P<0.05.

The MnNS:CDs were prepared through a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 A). In the process, SC acted as a carbon structure-directing agent owing to the intermolecular H-bonding while the NAC and Na2S afforded nitrogen and sulfur dopants. Mn ions was supplied by manganese (II) acetate. The reactants undergo polymerization and carbonization under the high temperature and pressure, expediting heteroatom doping and the formation of MnNS:CDs with new optical and electronic properties differ from pristine CDs. Microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction with shorter reaction time, control over reaction parameters, low energy consumption, and absence of a temperature gradient is advantageous in the simultaneous carbonization and passivation with multiple precursors. As shown in Figs. 1B and 1C, the MnNS:CDs are dispersed quasi-spherical particles with an average particle size of 3.8 ± 0.1 nm. HR-TEM image (insets of Fig. 1B) revealed a crystalline structure of the h-CDs having a lattice fringe distance of 0.21 nm corresponding to (100) facets of graphitic carbon [44]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy in Fig. 1D shows the presence of Mn in MnNS:CDs, an indication of successful incorporation of Mn species. Further structural characterization of MnNS:CDs by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1E) showed the crystalline G band, which is related to the in-plane vibration of sp 2 carbons, and the disorder D band that indicates the presence of sp 3 defects. The value of the ID/IG ratio of 1.47 means the presence of defects due to the heteroatom doping [45].

The optical behavior of the MnNS:CDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy. An absorbance profile with distinct peaks at 260 nm and 340 nm was observed (Fig. 2 A). The peak at 260 nm is ascribed to
π −
π *
transition in the C = C bond of the aromatic ring. The peak at 340 nm is assigned to
n −
π *
transition and attributed to C = O bonds as well as Mn, N, and S related surface defects [34,46]. With an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, a maximum emission at 437 nm was recorded for MnNS:CDs (Fig. 2B). First, the effects of single and co-doping on the PL emission were studied systematically. As shown in Fig. S1, the PL emission was significantly improved upon dual N and S doping when compared to the single S doping. However, increasing NAC concentration in the reaction tube led to a lower QY of NS:CDs, which might be attributed to the aggregation of CDs and subsequent self-quenching effect. Likewise, an increase in the Mn content leads to variation in absorption intensity at 340 nm and the corresponding emission spectra. The optimized condition for the highest QY of 17.7% was achieved with 0.1 mmol initial Mn precursor. The Mn concentration was estimated for the obtained MnNS:CDs using ICP measurements (Table S1). The quenching effect at higher concentrations of Mn has probably resulted from coordination interactions between Mn2+ and the oxygenous groups on the MnNS:CDs owing to the enhanced charge-transfer and subsequence non-radiative recombination [47]. Furthermore, the PL emission of the MnNS:CDs showed no significant shift as the excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm were applied (Figs. 2C and S2), featuring an excitation-independent emission. In order to obtain more insights into the photophysical dynamics of h-CDs, the PL decay studied as a function of time at room temperature. The decay profiles fitted according to the first exponential function (Eq. (4)):
(4)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
− t /
τ 1
)
where I(t) is the photon intensity, A 1 is the amplitude, and τ 1 is the decay time. The time-resolved PL curves indicated that NS:CDs and MnNS:CDs had almost similar decay lifetimes of 5.9 ± 0.1, and 5.4 ± 0.1 ns, respectively (Fig. 2D). Favorably, the MnNS:CDs showed higher PL stability than NS:CDs after being subjected to UV irradiation and monitored for 170 min (Fig. 2E). The former exhibited less than 15% PL quenching while NS:CDs showed more than 30% drop in PL intensity. The stability of the PL emission was also investigated in the presence of 0 to 2 M NaCl. As can be seen in Figs. 2F and S3, a slight quenching effect was observed after 72 h at room temperature, demonstrating good stability, which is necessary for further biological applications.

The chemical and elemental compositions of MnNS:CDs investigated by XPS, which in combination with EDS data confirmed that the MnNS:CDs were primarily composed of C, O, N, S, and Mn (Fig. 3 A). The atomic percentage of these elements was calculated from XPS measurement and listed in Table S2. As shown in Figs. 3B and 3C, the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of MnNS:CDs were deconvoluted into several peaks corresponding to C = C, C–C, C–S, C–O, C–N, C–OH, and O–C = O that suggest the presence of oxygeneous groups as well as N and S dopants [48]. The Mn 2p spectrum of MnNS:CDs displayed an asymmetric Mn 2p 3/2 peak at 638.7 ± 0.1 eV with a 2p 3/2 to 2p 1/2 splitting of 11.8 ± 0.1 eV (Fig. 3D). This compares well to previous works and the NIST database for metallic Mn [49]. There is also a distinct shoulder developed on the higher energy side of the peak that may represent Mn in higher oxidation states [49].

The incorporation of Mn ions in the MnNS:CDs was further confirmed by EPR spectrometry measurement, which provides information about the characteristics of the dopants in the local environment inside the host. Fig. 3E shows the EPR spectra of MnNS:CDs prepared by different Mn2+ concentrations, ranging from 0.01 to 0.04 mmol. It is clear that EPR intensity increased in response to the higher level of Mn contents. The six Mn hyperfine structure lines in the EPR spectra for MnNS:CDs are due to the electron-spin and nuclear-spin interactions of Mn [50]. The g values are 1.90, 1.93, 1.99, 2.04, 2.11, and 2.14, which match well to those reported in the literature for Mn2+ dopants [50-52].

To validate the possible use of MnNS:CDs as a diagnostic contrast agent, a 7T clinical MRI system was used to evaluate the longitudinal (T1) and transverse (T2) relaxation times. The T1- and T2-weighted MR images of aqueous dispersions with serial dilutions of MnNS:CDs (0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.1, and 1.3 mM of Mn) showed shortening of the T1 relaxation time, and higher T1 and lower T2 MR signals with increasing Mn2+ concentration (Figs. 3F and 3G). To quantitatively explore the ability of the MnNS:CDs MR signal enhancement, the longitudinal (r 1) and transverse (r 2) relaxivity values were determined from the slope of relaxation time (1/T) against the Mn2+ concentrations plots. The r 1 and r 2 values for MnNS:CDs were 5.30 and 27.9 mM−1s−1, respectively. The MRI of Mn(CH3COO)2 at the same Mn concentrations was studied as a control group. As shown in Fig. S4, the estimated r 1 value of 11.0 mM−1s−1 was higher than that of MnNS:CDs. However, a T2-weighted signal decay was observed at higher concentrations of manganese acetate, limiting the concentration range where dual-modal MRI enhancement can be acquired. Similar results were reported for manganese chloride because of Mn clusters formation at high levels [53]. While the toxicity of free Mn2+ is a bottleneck for in vivo application [54,55], MnNS:CDs might serve as dual-modal MRI/fluorescent imaging agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of ROS and RNS scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para: The radical scavenging properties of the as-prepared h-CDs were first examined against the DPPH model radical. As shown in Figs. S5, 4A and 4B, DPPH characteristic peak at 515 nm decreased as the concentration of ascorbic acid, a commercial antioxidant, and h-CDs in the solution increased. Fig. S6 presents photographs of the DPPH reaction solutions where a steady decrease in the solution's red color was observed upon reaction with higher concentrations of MnNS:CDs. The inhibition values for DPPH radicals are then calculated and summarized in Fig. 4 C. Results suggest that the h-CDs can serve as a moderate scavenger of the DPPH radicals. The value of half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) for ascorbic acid was estimated as 2.41 µg/mL, while the IC50 values for NS:CDs and MnNS:CDs were calculated as 10.56 and 13.59 µg/mL, respectively.

Furthermore, TMB assay employed to evaluate the OH radical scavenging ability of h-CDs. The OH radicals in the TMB assay, are generated by the Fenton reaction in the presence of H2O2 and Fe2+ [39]. Similarly, Figs. 4D and 4E show a continuous decline in the solution absorption at 652 nm with increasing h-CDs concentration from 0 to 50 µg/mL. The absorption peak at 652 nm is due to the formation of blue oxidized TMB after the reaction of TMB with •OH. At higher levels of h-CDs, OH radicals in the solution were scavenged, which is reflected in the lower absorption value and decay of the oxidized TMB blue color (Fig. S6). The IC50 value extracted from Fig. 4F for MnNS:CDs (10.33 µg/mL) is lower than NS:CDs (20.50 µg/mL) and comparable to that of ascorbic acid (8.00 µg/mL). We further investigated the ROS scavenging of the nanoenzyme against O2 •¯. As shown in Fig. S7, slight inhibition was observed in the range of 10- 50 µg/mL, reaching up to only 33% inhibition at 50 µg/mL of MnNS:CDs. Additionally, the RNS scavenging capability of the nanoenzyme was evaluated for the peroxynitrite ion clearance. As can be seen from Fig. 4G, 98% of ONOO− can be cleared within 30 min after reaction with 100 µg/mL of MnNS:CDs. The absorption peak at 302 nm from ONOO− appreciably declined in response to the increment of MnNS:CDs content and reaction time (Figs. 4H and 4I). As concentration increased to 20 µg/mL, 50% decrease in the absorption was observed, indicating a moderate scavenging activity against ONOO−.

As heteroatom-doped CDs, the MnNS:CDs are rich in functional groups and anticipated to exhibit scavenging effects against a wide range of free radicals. The antioxidant studies indicate that MnNS:CDs nanoenzyme can neutralize ROS and RNS, offering higher selectivity toward hydroxyl radicals.

The MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates were prepared through EDC/Sulfo-NHS coupling chemistry, in which activated carboxylic acid groups on the MnNS:CDs surface formed amide bonds with the pre-amine-functionalized HA (Fig. 5 A). In the procedure, the carboxyl group on HA reacts with EDC, and a highly reactive but unstable O-acylisourea intermediate is generated. Upon reaction with Sulfo-NHS, the intermediate can be transformed into a water-soluble amine-reactive NHS ester, and undergoes nucleophilic substitution with the EBEA primary amines to form amine-functionalized HA (HA-NH2). The UV and FT-IR spectra of HA and HA-NH2 were compared in Fig. S8. A new UV absorption bands at 243 nm can be assigned to the double bonds of carbonyl and carboxyl groups after activation and amide bond formation [56]. Noticeably, the HA-NH2 spectrum revealed an absorption feature at ~340 nm, which was absent for the HA solution. Similarly, carboxylic acid groups on the MnNS:CDs surface were activated and reacted with amine-terminated HA to form MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates through amide bonds. From the comparison of the UV–vis absorbance spectra of MnNS:CDs and MnNS:CDs@HA (Fig. 5B), the appearance of 348 nm peak in the MnNS:CDs@HA spectrum confirmed the conjugation of HA to the MnNS:CDs.

The FT-IR spectrum was acquired to determine the surface groups and to verify the amine-functionalization of HA and its conjugation to the MnNS:CDs. Comparison of HA and HA-NH2 FT-IR spectra confirmed the amine-functionalization of HA (Fig. S8), as the corresponding vibrations of -OH and NH broad bands at 3398 cm−1, CH at 2930 cm−1, C=O at 1692 cm−1, CO at 1640 cm−1, and NH 1563 cm−1 have altered after modification [57]. Accordingly, additional peaks attributed to HA-NH2 were observed in the MnNS:CDs@HA. The spectrum for the MnNS:CDs showed major peaks at 3408, 2936, 1602, 1404, and 1116 cm−1 (Fig. 5C). The broad absorption bands at 3408 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of OH, indicating that there are many hydroxyl groups on the surface of the MnNS:CDs. The stretching vibration present at 2936 cm−1 is assigned to -C-H groups on the surface. The bands at 1602 and 1404 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of conjugated C = O and CO bonds, respectively. The weak band observed at 1116 cm−1 is assigned to CO-C stretching vibrations. After HA conjugation, the 3408 cm−1 band of MnNS:CDs shifted to 3412 cm−1 in MnNS:CDs@HA. The feeble 2936 cm−1 band of MnNS:CDs shifted to 2955 cm−1 in MnNS:CDs@HA, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of CH. Similarly, the 1602 and 1404 cm−1 bands in MnNS:CDs shifted to 1614 and 1411 cm−1, respectively, in MnNS:CDs@HA, and new peaks that appeared at 1222 and 1072 cm−1 are assigned as stretching vibrations of the CN and COH functional groups arising from amid bonds.

As shown in Figs. 5D and 5E, after HA conjugation, the average hydrodynamic size was increased from 36 to 90 nm. Average zeta potential value of MnNS:CDs was –13.2 ± 0.4 mV at pH 7.4. The negative value is related to the MnNS:CDs surface functional groups such as –OH and COO-. Subsequently, conjugation of HA with MnNS:CDs led to a higher negative zeta potential of about –22.4 ± 0.3 mV (Fig. 5F), which is mostly due to the increased negative functional groups on the surface which in part improve water solubility and colloidal stability. Subsequently, colloidal and PL stability of MnNS:CDs@HA conjugate were investigated by dispersion in different media, including DI water, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), MES, and Eagle's minimum essential medium (EMEM). As shown in Fig. S9, the MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates were well dispersed in each medium without precipitation and aggregation. Interestingly, the blue emission under UV excitation was remained unchanged in all four media after dispersion for 21 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo biocompatibility studies
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of MnNS:CDs and MnNS:CDs@HA conjugate against B16F1 and HeLa cell lines were investigated by WST-1 assay. It can be seen from Fig. S10, that the cell viability upon exposure to different concentrations (0–1000 µg/mL) of MnNSCDs for 24 h was more than 90%. As shown in Figs. 6 A and S10, the MnNS:CDs@HA displayed higher viability for both cancer cell lines (>96%). Besides, the biocompatibility of MnNS:CDs@HA was evaluated in vivo using a zebrafish model. The zebrafish embryos were immersed in the MnNS:CDs@HA (0, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 µg/mL) aqueous solutions to allow their uptake by the embryos via crossing the chorion and the germ ring in the yolk [58]. The optical images captured during embryonic developmental stages of untreated zebrafish and of those treated with different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA are shown in Fig. S11. Likewise, the treated embryos revealed normal development for 3 hpf (cleavage), 24 hpf (segmentation), 48 hpf (hatching), and 96 hpf (developed larva) stages. The embryonic survival and hatching rate after incubation with different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA are shown in Fig. 6B. The overall survival rate of >90% and the hatching rate of >96% at the highest concentration indicate that MnNS:CDs@HA did not induce a noticeable mortality and hatching delay of the embryos. Thus, the MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates showed negligible cytotoxicity, which permits their future biomedical applications.

With our broader interest in the potential nanoenzyme activity, the therapeutic antioxidant effect was further examined by studying the cytoprotective effect of the as-prepared MnNS:CDs@HA against H2O2-induced damage on B16F1 cell line. As shown in Fig. 6C, H2O2 induced cell damage, which left only 17% and 12% viable cells upon exposure to 0.2 and 1 mM H2O2, respectively. Our results indicated that pre-treated B16F1 cells suffered less oxidative damage owing to the ROS scavenging activity of MnNS:CDs@HA. It is assumed that MnNS:CDs@HA prompts a dose-dependent cytoprotective effect, which is in line with the enhancement of cell viability as the MnNS:CDs@HA nanoenzyme concentration increased from 200 to 500 µg/mL. In fact, at higher levels of the MnNS:CDs@HA, the ROS scavenging was improved, and lower oxidative damage gave rise to more upper cell viability. For instance, pretreatment with MnNS:CDs@HA at 200 µg/mL led to 16% and 12% higher cell viability for the 0.2 and 1 mM H2O2 treatment groups, respectively. Likewise, increasing the nanoenzyme concentration up to 500 µg/mL could protect cells from H2O2-induced oxidative stress damage. As a result, cell viability rose to 24% and 19% for the 0.2 and 1 mM H2O2 treatment groups. Related to the mechanism of oxidation protection is the importance of sp 2 carbon sites that may involve radical adduct formation (Fig. 6D). These sites, in turn, delocalize spin across the conjugated graphene backbone and lead to the destruction of the radicals [19,29]. Furthermore, MnNS:CDs@HA contains Mn species and can act as a catalase mimic and reduce the oxidative-stress related damage by decomposing H2O2 to H2O and O2 [59,60].

The intercellular ROS inhibition was also monitored by DCFH-DA assay. Upon oxidation of DCFH-DA in the presence of ROS, an oxidized product, DCF with green fluorescence, is formed. According to the CLSM results in Fig. 7 , there was almost no fluorescence in the control group. Slight green fluorescence was observed for the DCFH-treated cells. Compared to the control group without hydrogen peroxide treatment, the addition of H2O2 in the cell medium induced a significant increase in the green fluorescence as an indicator of the intercellular ROS level. The greater the level of H2O2, the higher the green fluorescence signal. On the other hand, pretreatment of cells with MnNS:CDs@HA nanoparticles reduced the fluorescence suggesting a lower level of intercellular ROS. A moderate inhibitory effect on ROS generation was observed for 200 µg/mL of MnNS:CDs@HA, while treatment with 500 µg/mL significantly vanished the green signal after post-treatment with 0.2 mM H2O2. These results illustrated antioxidant activities against intercellular ROS induced by H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted bioimaging of MnNS:CDs@HA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of MnNS:CDs@HA for application in targeted cancer cell imaging, cellular uptake by B16F1 cells, which are characterized by their overexpression of the CD44 receptor, is compared with the CD44 deficient HeLa cells. The in vitro cellular uptake and internalization of MnNS:CDs@HA were observed by CLSM. The morphological integrity of the untreated B16F1 and HeLa cells were visualized by the bright-field images and the green fluorescence images of NucView-stained nuclei (Fig. 8 A). The blue fluorescence is attributed to the MnNS:CDs@HA that co-localized with a green signal indicating their cellular uptake. To further investigate the HA receptor-mediated cellular uptake of MnNS:CDs@HA, the B16F1 cells were pre-treated with free HA in order to block HA receptors. Consequently, a weaker blue emission was observed, suggesting competitive inhibition of MnNS:CDs@HA uptake due to the excess free HA molecules and their interaction with the B16F1 cells. Comparably, the blue fluorescent signal was much weaker in the HeLa cells with poor CD44-receptor expression than B16F1 cells. In the merged images, the blue fluorescent signals were also more dominant in the nuclei of the B16F1 cells than in those of the HeLa cells owing to the specific targeting of the MnNS:CDs@HA agents. The above results indicate that the cellular uptake of MnNS:CDs@HA by B16F1 cells was mainly through the HA receptor-mediated endocytosis.

To further evaluate the specific cancer cell-targeting ability, we incubated B16F1 and HeLa cells with different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA conjugate and examined the cells’ MR contrast images. The T1-weighted MR image of B16F1 cells incubated with MnNS:CDs@HA displayed a brighter contrast than did the HeLa cells incubated with the identical solution of the conjugate (Fig. 8B). There was no significant variation in the MR signals of HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA. These results indicate that MnNS:CDs@HA possessed high tumor selectivity on account of the HA receptor CD44-mediated endocytosis process. Taken together, the CLSM and MR imaging studies revealed that the MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates are appropriate for MR and fluorescence imaging, making them potential dual-mode probes for a more precise diagnosis. We also used ICP-AES to quantify the cellular uptake after incubation of different concentrations of MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates (Fig. S12). Results showed an average cellular internalization increase up to 500 µg/mL. However, incubation of B16F1 cells with a higher concentration of 1000 µg/mL did not significantly promote cellular uptake of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX delivery and release studies
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the potential of the fabricated MnNS:CDs@HA probe for targeted delivery, DOX as a model cancer chemotherapeutic drug was coupled with MnNS:CDs@HA to form the MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX theranostic agent (Fig. 8C), and its stimuli-responsive release was investigated. The loading efficiency of DOX into MnNS:CDs@HA was 72 ± 5%. The loading and releasing concentrations of DOX were calculated by referring to DOX calibration curves (Fig. S13) that had been established before sample measurement. The cumulative drug release from MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX was studied at different pH values, whereupon the process was shown to be pH-dependent (Fig. 8D). During the first 24 h, 58± 2%, 44 ± 1%, and 28 ± 1% of DOX were released at pH 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4, respectively. After 72 h, the cumulative release of DOX was 65.1 ± 0.8% at pH 5.0, 50 ± 3% at pH 6.0, and 30.2 ± 0.5% at pH 7.4. Evidently, at lower pH, the DOX release was higher.

Subsequently, the potential targeted chemotherapy effect of MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX towards B16F1 cells was assessed. In this regard, the cytotoxicity of free DOX and MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX were investigated in which B16F1 cells were incubated with their increasing concentrations (0–10 µg/mL) for 24 h and cell viability was measured using WST-1 assay. Inhibition of cell growth was dose-dependent for both free DOX and MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX. The corresponding cell killing percentages were 60% and 90% for the DOX concentration of 10 µg/mL. Thus, targeted accumulation of MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX efficiently suppressed the growth of B16F1 cells. The higher biocompatibility and targeting efficiency of MnNS:CDs@HA towards B16F1 cells, therefore, make it a practical imaging-guided drug delivery system to eradicate cancer cells.

CDs offer massive potentials in the design of artificial functional nanomaterials that mimic the structures and functions of natural fluorogens and enzymes with higher stability, scalability, and chemical diversity. The chemical functionality of CDs can be regulated by the reaction medium, precursors’ type, and dopants’ chemistry to determine the structure, surface states, and leaving groups, thereby fine-tune their photochemical and catalytic activity. For instance, the substitution of dopant atoms in the carbonaceous structure is accompanied by the introduction of defects, such as bonding disorders and vacancies in the graphene structure of CDs that, in turn, influence the spin density and the charge distribution of carbon atoms [61]. Such characteristics may alter the absorption and emission behavior of h-CDs. The quantum effect arising from the substantial surface-to-volume ratio of ultra-small CDs along with surface defects play a critical role in the engineering of their bandgap, which determines the PL emission [62]. Although the chemical structure and PL mechanism of MnNS:CDs cannot be entirely ruled out, it is clear that the absorption at about 260 nm corresponding to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp 2 domain was remained unchanged while the sp 3 hybridized domain varies with the amount of Mn in the h-CDs’ structure (Figs. 2A and 2B). Presumably, incorporation of Mn could improve the trapping of excited‐state energy via the newly formed surface states rather than electronic conjugate structures which resulted in strong emission. On the other hand, the new surface defects enhance the density of the catalytically active centers with low stereohindrance for binding redox species in catalytic reactions to serve as a nanoenzyme.

It has been reported that the N and S dual doping leads to the synergistic catalytic performance enhancement attributed to the redistribution of spin and charge densities, thus increasing the number of carbon atom active sites [63,64]. Favorably, the optimization of Mn content in MnNS:CDs could retain similar PL QY but exhibited higher •OH scavenging activity compared to the NS:CDs. A trade-off between two functions, namely, PL and catalytic activity was achieved by regulating Mn incorporation into the CD matrix. Guan et al. employed extended X-ray fine structure spectroscopy for characterization of Mn coordinates in Mn-embedded nitrogen-doped graphene catalyst, which revealed that Mn ion coordinated with four N atoms in the graphene matrix [65]. In another study, the Mn coordinations in the Mn-incorporated CDs were assigned to Mn–O/Mn–N stretching vibrations [66]. We speculate that some of the Mn2+ ions reacted with oxygenous groups giving rise to the formation of Mn-O with higher oxidation state in the MnNS:CDs, as reflected in the XPS spectra. Regarding the PL characteristic, the incorporated Mn likely contributes to the promotion of N and S states by reacting with oxygenous groups giving rise to the formation of manganese oxide species [16,63]. While N and S surface groups enhance PL intensity, the coexistence of Mn atoms and oxidized Mn bonding configurations can promote catalytic activities to directly oxidize hydrogen peroxide to produce water and oxygen [59,60]. Furthermore, the nanoscale dimension of MnNS:CDs offers a higher surface area that facilitates rapid diffusion of dissolved ROS and RNS to the active catalytic sites, accelerating nanoenzyme antioxidant characteristics [67]. Therefore, CDs with enzyme-like characteristics, represent an exciting and emerging research area in the field of nanoenzymes as they present some advantages over natural enzymes, including straightforward and low-cost preparation, high stability, and tunable catalytic activity.

Mn possesses five unpaired electrons in the 3d 5 high-spin configuration, and a trace of Mn element is necessary for the human body posing less toxicity than the commercially available gadolinium-based contrast. However, the ionic form of Mn2+ showed a short plasma half-life [68], while bound Mn2+ agents displayed a stronger relaxivity than its free ions [55]. The simultaneous T1 and T2 contrast enhancement effects, together with stable PL emission in simulated physiological conditions, propose the potential application of the biocompatible MnNS:CDs@HA as a targeted dual PL/MR imaging contrast agent to acquire doubly verified information for more precise diagnostics. HA shell not only enables specific targeting but also assists in aqueous stability and carrying a high DOX payload through non-covalent association. DLS measurement in Fig. S14 revealed that a slight drop in the PL intensity after 72 h might be related to the minor changes in the MnNS:CDs size distribution in 2 M NaCl solution. Favorably, a comparison of Figs. 5E and S14 showed that the formation of aggregates was negligible for the MnNS:CDs@HA sample in the 2 M salt solution. The geometry of nanocarriers can dictate the uptake mechanism, which, in turn, generates uncertainty about the cell-entry kinetics due to the complexity of the nano–bio interactions. Since the MnNS:CDs@HA conjugates showed good stability in different media, the lower cellular internalization at higher concentrations (Fig. S12) might be related to CD44-mediated endocytosis. As presented in Fig. 8A, the cellular uptake was significantly decreased by pretreatment of B16F1 cells with free HA. Considering the results of this study, blocking of CD44 receptors at the higher level of MnNS:CDs@HA was envisioned as an inhibitory effect for cellular internalization.

As shown in the schematic illustration of Fig. 8C, DOX coupling with MnNS:CDs@HA takes place via π–π stacking and probably hydrophobic interactions with CDs. It is advantageous over covalent modification that may lead to a loss of HA recognition after a multi-step modification process. Non-covalent encapsulation of DOX also benefits controlled release at acidic tumor environment as it is reflected in the elevated release and potent cell toxicity of MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX in Fig. 8D and 8E. The higher release rate of DOX at the mildly acidic pH is attributed to the increased solubility of protonated DOX in the endosomal environment that weakens its interaction with MnNS:CDs@HA related to the increased protonation of the HA-NH2 and DOX molecules [69-71]. The pH-dependent release of DOX from MnNS:CDs@HA/DOX proposes its use as a multifunctional platform for the clinical chemotherapeutic application.

With the enhanced biocompatibility and specific targeting, MnNS:CDs@HA exhibited great potential as dual-modal nanoprobes with antioxidant therapeutic effect. While enhanced permeation and retention and receptor-mediated targeting allow passive and active accumulation of nanoparticles, there is also concurrent accumulation, though less prominent, in some other tissues that raised toxicity concern. Although the fate of nanomaterials and their adverse effects have not been adequately studied, it is suggested that the high surface area may contribute to the oxidation of cell membranes [72]. Using nanoprobes and nanocarriers with antioxidant properties might be considered a possible solution to address such potential risks or to further curb and combat oxidation related diseases while they are circulating in the body fluid.

In summary, a straightforward one-pot microwave-assisted approach was developed to prepare h-CDs as a theranostic platform to serve as a fluorescence/magnetic resonance dual-modal imaging probe, catalase mimic for free radical scavenging, and targeted delivery and release of DOX. The whole synthetic procedure is rapid, efficiently reproducible, and scalable leading to the MnNS:CDs@HA nanoenzymes with ROS and RNS scavenging activity and biocompatibility which verified in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, in vitro cell toxicity results showed that targeted delivery and pH-dependent release of DOX from the theranostic MnNS:CDs@HA nanocarrier give rise to much higher chemotherapeutic effect toward B16F1 as compared to free DOX treatment group. This study exemplifies the engineering of multifunctional CDs with nanoenzyme like properties, which is an intriguing topic in nanomedicine and opens up immense opportunities for non-invasive multi-modal imaging and therapy.
